raw_consumer_statement: On XX/XX/year> we agreed to pay {$10000.00} USD to a travel agency in XXXX XXXX using a Citibank XXXX  credit card. The travel agency converted the USD amount to the local currency ( XXXX ) and calculated that amount to be XXXX XXXX which was an accurate conversion based on all currency conversion indexes. When Citibank XXXX  posted the charge to our account, the amount came to USD XXXX ( a difference of {$700.00} ). This USD amount was 8.5 % higher than the accurate conversion. We called Citibank multiple times and spoke to multiple people to try and understand why the conversion was not accurate or even close. We were handed off to different people in different departments from different call centers and were eventually told that XXXX and not Citibank calculated the incorrect conversion rate. After multiple attempts, we were eventually given the phone number to XXXX XXXX. The person at XXXX then explained to us that Citibank actually establishes the conversion rate and not XXXX XXXX. Regardless of which company was at fault, the Truth in Lending Act clearly spells out transparency with costs and charges related to credit card transactions. The Citibank credit card agreement offers a vague explanation and states : " A XXXX XXXX converts the amount of a Purchase in a foreign currency into XXXX dollars. Each XXXX XXXX follows its own procedures for conversion ''. That is not Truth in Lending and consumers do not even know what the " XXXX XXXX '' even means. Without proper disclosure of fees, the consumer can not make the decision to use or not use a credit card for a foreign transaction. Either Citibank or XXXX needs to be held accountable for the lack of transparency. If this policy is allowed to exist, companies are given the green light to charge any rate without the consumer knowing the consequences.
